data.title: Amazon: Just Like Google In 2023
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock has failed to impress despite AWS (Amazon Web Services) accelerating over 400 basis points sequentially in Q4 FY25, while its custom silicon, specifically Trainium and Graviton, now has a combined annual revenue run rate exceeding $10B.

In fact, just a few weeks after the company’s Q4 earnings, OpenAI announced an $110B funding round where Amazon would be investing $50B in a multi-year strategic partnership with the company. As part of this deal, OpenAI committed 2 gigawatts of Trainium capacity while expanding the original $38B AWS compute agreement from November by $100B over eight years, directly adding to AWS’s top line.

This news too failed to move the needle on the stock, as investors remain squarely focused on the question of Amazon’s capex sustainability.

In the company’s latest earnings, Amazon management raised their FY26 capex guidance by over 50% to $200B, which might lead to their free cash flow turning negative, as the company would now have to tap into the debt markets to fund its AI buildout ambitions.

The way I see it, Amazon looks quite similar to Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) in early 2023. In 2023, investors were heavily skeptical that Google might face extensive disruption to its search monopoly from OpenAI’s ChatGPT. Yet, no one was paying attention to the work Google was quietly doing to control the entire AI value chain with its custom TPUs and utilizing them to build Gemini and optimizing the performance of its services (Search, YouTube, etc.).

Amazon today is in a similar spot, where it is fiercely doubling down on its custom silicon roadmap, which in turn is helping it accelerate its AWS revenue growth as enterprises look to diversify away from Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) GPUs, while Bedrock creates a powerful ecosystem lock-in in the AWS stack.

Unfortunately, investors are looking past it all and may only be convinced when they see a bottoming in the company’s free cash flow to re-rate the stock higher. At the moment, long-term investors, such as myself, need to remain patient with this company, as the risk-reward continues to remain attractive.

Fierce Custom Silicon Strategy Keeps AWS Acceleration Intact

Amazon’s AWS revenue accelerated yet again in Q4 FY25, growing 24% YoY, and it does not look like it will stop anytime soon.

During the quarter, AWS revenue reached an annualized run rate of $142B, with their custom silicon business, including Graviton (custom CPU) and Trainium (custom XPU/accelerator), at a $10B annual revenue run rate, growing triple-digit percentages year-over-year, and backlog reaching $244B, representing a year-on-year increase of 40%.

A few weeks after Amazon reported its earnings, OpenAI announced an $110B funding round, with Amazon investing $50B in a multi-year strategic partnership with the company.

Note this is not an extension of the November deal, where OpenAI had committed $38B over seven years to rent NVIDIA GPU capacity on AWS. In the latest strategic partnership between the two companies, Amazon has skin in the game, unlike before, with its $50B investment, which will be broken into two chunks consisting of $15B upfront, followed by $35B conditional on certain requirements being met.

Not only that, while the November deal was explicitly about renting Nvidia capacity on AWS, the latest deal has OpenAI committing 2 gigawatts of Trainium capacity, which is a strong validation of Amazon’s custom silicon strategy, along with AWS becoming the exclusive third-party cloud distribution provider for OpenAI’s enterprise agent platform called Frontier.

Finally, the latest deal also expands the original $38B AWS compute agreement by $100B over eight years, directly adding to AWS’s top line, as OpenAI boosts its forward revenue target to $280B by 2030, up from $20B in 2025, a feat that would require at least $600B in infrastructure spending on OpenAI’s part.

What really strikes me is Amazon’s fierce push of its custom silicon in order to set itself apart. During the earnings call, Trainium (which is their custom accelerator) was mentioned 27 times, while Nvidia was not mentioned at all. This is what management said below that demonstrates the nature of the force in which they are ramping up their Trainium2 chips while preparing for the future generations of custom accelerators.

“We've landed over 1.4 million Trainium2 chips, our fastest ramping chip launch ever. Trainium2 is 30% to 40% more price-performant than comparable GPUs and is a multibillion-dollar annualized revenue run rate business with 100,000-plus companies using it, as Trainium is the majority underpinning of Bedrock usage today. We recently launched Trainium3, which is up to 40% more price-performant than Trainium2. We're seeing very strong demand for Trainium3, and expect nearly all of our Trainium3 supply of chips to be committed by mid-2026. And though we're still building Trainium4, we're seeing very strong interest already.”

The same is true for their custom CPU silicon, Graviton, which management mentioned is up to 40% more price performant than leading x86 processors and is used by 90% of top AWS’s 1000 customers.

In fact, Anthropic has already been training its latest Claude model on Trainium2 chips in Amazon’s Project Rainier cluster, which is one of the primary reasons behind the reacceleration in AWS revenue, which I have discussed in my previous posts.

With the latest commitment from OpenAI to consume 2GW of Trainium capacity along with $100B worth of compute spend on AWS, Goldman expects that the two AI labs could generate a combined AWS revenue of $13.2B in 2026, which could potentially reach $60.1B by 2029, accounting for over 20% of total AWS revenue.

The way I see it, Amazon’s fierce push of its custom silicon roadmap is starting to look a lot like Google’s vertical integration strategy, where the company owns its full hardware and software stack with its TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) that it built to optimize its own services such as Search, YouTube, etc., while bypassing the “Nvidia tax” to drive superior price-performance. While Google originally reserved TPUs for internal projects, they are now widely used by external companies and are one of the primary reasons why Google Cloud has been accelerating higher, as enterprises seek lower-cost alternatives to Nvidia’s GPUs.

We are now starting to see a similar pattern play out for Amazon AWS, as the company is rapidly accelerating their custom silicon release cadences with Trainium3 and Trainium4 while offering an end-to-end platform that is tied to the AWS ecosystem.

Capex Altering Free Cash Flow Dynamics Concerns Investors

Yet, despite the acceleration in AWS, a trend that I do not expect to slow for the next several quarters, investors remain concerned.

In fact, the stock has barely moved over the last year, despite its revenue and earnings per share growing 12.38% and 28.84%, respectively.

This is similar to Google in the first part of 2023, when the stock struggled relative to other hyperscalers as investors feared that Google’s search monopoly would be disrupted by ChatGPT, especially when the company announced Code Red along with several regulatory headwinds that it was facing at that time.

In the case of Amazon, investor skepticism is tied to the company’s full-year capex guidance, which is expected to grow over 50% YoY to $200B in FY26. However, it is not just Amazon that has massively raised its full-year capex guidance, but also Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Meta (NASDAQ:META) and Google, which are now looking to invest roughly $680B in AI capex for 2026 (including Amazon), roughly double that of the FY25 figure.

The reason why such an elevated level of capex is concerning is because it alters the free cash flow mechanics. In the case of Amazon, its free cash flow is expected to turn negative this year, as its operating cash flow will not grow fast enough to cover the planned $200B in capex in FY26. This would mean that the AI buildout, which was once funded by self-sustaining operating cash flows, will now require Amazon tapping into the debt markets, like it did recently with its largest corporate bond offering, raising approximately $54B earlier this month.

Yet, the encouraging sign is the backlog at Amazon has been growing at an accelerated rate over the last two quarters, with CEO Andy Jassy reiterating that they are "monetizing capacity as fast as we can install it," suggesting that the conversion of the backlog into revenue is accelerating as they add capacity.

What is also important to note is that investors calculating return on capex utilizing current revenue estimates is fundamentally flawed. As Rihard Jarc from New Era Funds points out, the capex a hyperscaler spends on a data center in a given year will be utilized over a 2-year period, as it takes around 2 years to build and operationalize a data center.

Therefore, when we are looking at 2025 revenue growth for hyperscalers, in this case, Amazon, we should translate that into capex spent in 2023, not in 2024 or 2025. The same is true for 2026 capex, which will translate into revenue two years down the line. At the moment, consensus estimates are pointing to revenue upwards of $1T in FY28, which is being revised higher, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang also firmly defended the $3-4T of AI capex buildout over the next several years, as I discussed in my Oracle post last week. According to him, the AI buildout is necessary in order to handle the exponential growth in tokenization as we enter the Agentic AI era.

“In this new world of AI, compute equals revenues. Without compute, there’s no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there’s no way to grow revenues. [...] I am certain at this point that we’ve reached the inflection point.”

Valuation & Conclusions

Amazon is currently trading at a non-GAAP forward PE ratio of 27.5. When we take FY28 earnings per share expectations into account, the forward PE ratio compresses to under 18, which is slightly below the 10-year average of the S&P 500.

As I explained below, investors are heavily skeptical of the altering free cash flow dynamics from the surging capex while also discounting the risk of failed or delayed revenue realization from the backlog, which would further strain margins and free cash flow.

The way I see it, until investors see a bottoming in free cash flow margins, chances are that investor pessimism may linger, despite AWS likely accelerating its top line growth in the coming quarters.

On a technical basis, if the stock is able to close above its 100 DMA at $227, it would likely head higher towards $240 per share. Whether it reaches an all-time high in the near term is difficult to say at the moment amid heightened scrutiny on AI capex as well as geopolitical uncertainty that has been plaguing the S&P 500.

What is encouraging is that Wall Street’s average price target of $280 per share suggests a 33% upside, compared to the downside risk of 16% to the lowest price target of $175 per share, as can be seen below.

As a result, I will continue to hold my position in Amazon at current levels but might trim into strength to reduce the size of my exposure in it depending on the evolving market narrative at the time.

Note that aside from their AWS business, Amazon derives 82% of its revenue from its North America and International Segments, which are heavily subject to macroeconomic volatility. Plus, it remains to be seen how Amazon manages resource allocation and its custom silicon roadmap now that it holds strategic investments in two competing AI labs, which is a great hedge on paper but could be a lot more nuanced in practice.

data.title: Hudson Pacific: Why You Have To Look Past The 6X FFO Multiple
data.description:
In our previous coverage of Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP) we explained why the common shares were not yet on the pink sheets. Our conclusion did not have any cheers for the long-suffering bulls, and this time we extended our warnings up the capital structure.

This is a fairly unusual situation where management has shown fortitude to dilute this late in the game. But we don't think this saves the day. The headwinds are too big. At this point, we are maintaining our Strong Sell ratings on the stock and the preferred. We are also suggesting a full-scale "abandon ship" on the bonds. They were priced for distress, but despite the equity raise, we don't see them as attractive.

Source: Something Happened On The Way To Heaven

The common shares have headed in the right direction,

but the Hudson Pacific Properties, Inc. 4.750% CUM PFD C (HPP.PR.C) have been more resilient (down just 12%). We review the recently released Q4-2025 and update our outlook.

Q4-2025

HPP's early data slide from their presentation gives everyone 80% of the information they need to abandon ship. We show the slide below with highlighted portions and discuss them next.

The key takeaways there are,

1) Office occupancy rates are still falling.

2) While more leases were executed this quarter, the rents are really, really low ($41.41 a square foot).

3) Studio occupancy looks horrid. Some might blame this on the LLM mania, but we think that factor has nothing to do with it.

4) Adjusted funds from operations were negative 14 cents, despite a massive share count increase from last year.

5) Debt to EBITDA looks a bit better than last year, thanks to some asset sales and the equity raise. But the 11.2X is still so far into the crimson tide, that no one is sleeping easy.

6) The weighted average debt maturity is a tiny 2.6 years.

The key context on the rents we mentioned can be seen below. Most of HPP's maturing rents are so far below that $41.41 that it will literally go under even if it improves its occupancy levels while letting rents drop. We have highlighted a few of the quarters and years below, and 2028 is the final deadline for this company.

To be fair, HPP has some great assets. The recently completed Washington 1000 is a prime example of a trophy asset. Well, about as "trophy" as any office asset can be in today's market.

HPP just finished building this, and based on their renting site, the entire building is vacant.

Ok, almost the entire building is vacant.

We will note that the building was completed more than a year back, and there are just no bids for spaces. We are sure HPP management was hoping for a strong showing from this asset to help them out.

Outlook

Management had a positive outlook.

Following our significant derisking in 2025, our priorities are clear and executable: drive occupancy growth to unlock embedded NOI expansion, eliminate Quixote's earnings drag by year-end, and maintain capital discipline through value-driven assets, sales and strategic de-leveraging. On our capital recycling, we sold Element LA in the fourth quarter at a strong valuation. In 2026, we're targeting $200 million to $300 million of additional sales, while prioritizing transactions that are FFO-accretive through further de-leveraging.

For example, we're currently marketing 10900 and 10950 Washington in Culver City, which we successfully re-entitled for 508 residential units, and we have a very strong buyer and joint venture interest throughout.

Source: HPP Q4-2025 Conference Call Transcript

They are also forecasting a solid FFO number for the year and rising occupancies.

Turning to our 2026 outlook. Due to the progress Victor and Mark described, we are reinstating full year FFO guidance at $0.96 to $1.06 per diluted share. We anticipate slightly lower FFO in the first quarter relative to the fourth quarter 2025, followed by steady sequential growth throughout the year as our leasing pipeline converts to cash flow.

We are introducing annual average in-service office occupancy guidance of 80% to 82%. Clearly, our year-end occupancy will exceed this range.

Source: HPP Q4-2025 Conference Call Transcript

This will create a big turnaround from the net operating income drop we saw in 2025.

But the elephant in the room is still the debt. The $2.7 billion of debt (at HPP's share) is about 8 times the current common equity market capitalization. That maturity breakdown is also about as ominous as it gets.

You will note points number 4 and 7 below as well.

Management was pressed on this, but we did not get a definitive answer on that massive CMBS pool up for refinancing.

Blaine Heck Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division

Great. That's all very helpful. Just a couple of questions on the upcoming CMBS maturity on the Hollywood Media Portfolio. Can you just talk about kind of the tenor of those conversations with the lenders. Do you see an extension as a potential outcome? And would you expect to need an equity infusion of any sort for the refinancing or potential extension there?

Victor Coleman Chairman & CEO

So Blaine, we're unable to just discuss specifics over our loan extension negotiations because currently today, there's ongoing dialogue, and we're focused on the best outcome for us and our shareholders, including the respect of capital allocation. So we're not going to get into an open dialogue on a conference call as to the status, but we're in constant communication, and we're happy with the progress so far.

Source: HPP Q4-2025 Conference Call Transcript

Our sense is that this will require a 50% paydown. Half of that could come from select asset sales and the other half from yet another massive equity raise. And if they do all that, the first video game wave of the bad guys (read debt maturities) will be dealt with. Every single year is like that till 2029.

So you can sense our hesitation in trying to bottom-fish this.

From our first sell rating, the stock is down nearly 90%.

That is despite a rather unanticipated equity offering late in the game. A major real estate player took a big share of that, leading many investors to ask, "What are they seeing that the bears are missing?" Unfortunately, arguments from authority almost never work, and their investment is down 62% since then.

Even if the equity survives, we think the dilution required will create an effective wipeout. Effective wipeout here means another 90% price drop, adjusted, of course, for the inevitable stock splits. Just refinancing the entire capital stack at 7% rates means a wipeout. HPP bonds currently yield between 8% and 11%.

Those getting excited at the "6X FFO" should know that translates into $65 million of cash flow. $65 million won't even make a dent in the $2.7 billion of debt. So we are going to maintain the ratings with the caveat that the stock is very oversold, and a rebound to $8 could certainly happen on its way to $1.00.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it sounds like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Europe Can Regain Its Productivity Edge By Scaling Up
data.description:
By Diego Cerdeiro, deputy unit chief in the Regional Studies unit of the IMF’s European department | Stephan Danninger, Head, IMF’s Regional Studies Unit for Europe | Morgan Maneely, Senior Research Officer in the Advanced Economies unit of the IMF’s European Department

Capital markets integration, expanding opportunities for workers, and bigger consumer markets will allow companies to grow faster

Europe once led the world in productivity growth but now lags the United States — and the gap has widened significantly in recent years.

The Chart of the Week shows that behind this shortfall is the staggering difficulty that European firms face in scaling up. In the United States, the stock market valuation of young firms (under the age of 50) is $42.9 trillion, compared to a meager $5 trillion in the European Union.

This reflects imperfections in European integration. For all the achievements of the European single market, capital flows remain fragmented along national lines, opportunities for workers are hampered by regulations, and it is often difficult to market products across borders. The result is that the EU has too many small, old, and low-growth companies. The average European firm that has been in business for 25 years or more employs about 10 workers. A comparable US company employs 70 people.

It is therefore no surprise that Europe’s labor productivity levels are about 20 percent below those of the United States.

Our research shows that addressing this issue requires integration at various levels: of capital markets, to allow more funding to go to risky new businesses; of labor markets, to allow people to move to opportunity; and of consumer markets, so that companies can sell to bigger markets. The good news is that these are all changes that Europe can bring about.
